name: Via Augusta and Sierras de Altaona Loop
name_original: San Pedro del Pinatar - Estación de Canteras - Venta Los Pinos — circular desde San Javier
description: Cycle the Via Augusta and Sierras de Altaona Loop, a moderate 46.2-mile racebike route with 2015 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around San Javier benefit from a favorable climate and varied landscapes. The region features flat, fast routes along the Mar Menor coastline and the unique La Manga del Mar Menor land strip, allowing cycling between two seas. Inland, routes transition to quiet countryside with rolling hills and gentle climbs, providing options for more challenging rides. Natural parks and reserves, such as Salinas y Arenales de San Pedro del Pinatar, offer scenic paths with views of salt flats and lagoons. Best road cycling routes around San Javier The most popular road cycling route is Via Augusta and Sierras de Altaona Loop, a 46.2 miles (74.3 km) trail that takes 4 hours 13 minutes to complete. This moderate route features significant elevation gain through inland hills. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Portmán Coastal Road – Blick auf das Mar Menor loop from La Ribera - Iglesia, a moderate 46.0 miles (74.1 km) path. This route offers coastal views along the Mar Menor. Local road cyclists also love the La Pedrera Reservoir – La Pedrera Reservoir loop from San Javier, a 50.6 miles (81.4 km) trail leading through inland terrain with reservoir views, often completed in about 3 hours 45 minutes. Road cycling around San Javier is defined by the Mar Menor coastline, the unique La Manga del Mar Menor, and rolling inland hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from flat coastal rides to routes with continuous slopes. The routes in San Javier are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from more than 40 reviews. More than 1,800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore San Javier's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of road cycling routes can I find in San Javier?
A: San Javier offers a diverse range of road cycling routes. You'll find flat, fast rides along the Mar Menor coastline, unique experiences cycling on the La Manga del Mar Menor land strip between two seas, and more challenging inland routes with gentle climbs through quiet countryside and rolling hills. The region provides options for various ability levels, from easy coastal spins to routes with continuous slopes.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the San Javier area?
A: There are over 50 road cycling routes around San Javier, catering to different skill levels. This includes approximately 19 easy routes, 28 moderate routes, and 3 more difficult options for experienced cyclists.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in San Javier?
A: San Javier boasts an excellent climate with around 320 days of sunshine annually, making it suitable for road cycling almost year-round. Mild temperatures create ideal conditions, especially for pre-season training. Early mornings are particularly pleasant, offering calm air and beautiful golden light over the Mar Menor lagoon.

Q: Are there any routes that offer significant climbs or elevation gain?
A: Yes, while many coastal routes are flat, San Javier also offers inland options with significant elevation gain. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1010173758/via-augusta-and-sierras-de-altaona-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Via Augusta and Sierras de Altaona Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route with over 600 meters of elevation through inland hills. Routes towards areas like Garrucha, Cresta del Gallo, and Cabezo de la Plata also provide continuous slopes for experienced riders.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in San Javier?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in San Javier are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39563452" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Pedrera Reservoir – La Pedrera Reservoir loop from San Javier&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through inland terrain with reservoir views.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling routes in San Javier offer views of diverse natural features. You can cycle along the Mar Menor coastline, experience the unique La Manga del Mar Menor land strip, or explore inland areas with rolling hills. Notable natural attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/620881" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marchamalo Salt Flats&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5410964" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salt Flats and Sand Dunes of San Pedro del Pinatar&lt;/a&gt;, which offer scenic paths and opportunities for birdwatching.

Q: Are there any specific routes that offer coastal views of the Mar Menor?
A: Absolutely. The Mar Menor coastline is a defining feature of road cycling in San Javier. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5296921" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portmán Coastal Road – Blick auf das Mar Menor loop from La Ribera - Iglesia&lt;/a&gt; offer stunning coastal views along Europe's largest saltwater lagoon.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in San Javier?
A: The road cycling routes in San Javier are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.1 stars from over 40 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from flat coastal rides to challenging inland climbs, and the beautiful scenery, especially along the Mar Menor.

Q: Are there any unique attractions near the cycling routes, like mud baths?
A: Yes, a unique natural attraction near some cycling routes is the therapeutic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3920897" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mud Baths at Villananitos Beach&lt;/a&gt;. These mud baths, formed by centuries of sun and salty Mar Menor water, offer a distinctive experience for relaxation after a ride.

Q: Can I find routes that pass by significant bodies of water?
A: Many routes in San Javier feature views of the Mar Menor, Europe's largest saltwater lagoon. Additionally, inland routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39563452" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Pedrera Reservoir – La Pedrera Reservoir loop from San Javier&lt;/a&gt; offer scenic views of reservoirs and other water bodies.

Q: Are there any routes that allow me to cycle between two seas?
A: Yes, the distinctive La Manga del Mar Menor land strip offers the rare experience of cycling between the Mar Menor lagoon and the Mediterranean Sea. This 18 km stretch provides a unique geographical feature to explore on two wheels.

Q: Are there options for less experienced road cyclists?
A: Yes, San Javier offers a good selection of routes suitable for less experienced road cyclists. Approximately 19 of the routes are classified as easy, providing flatter terrain, especially along the Mar Menor coastline, perfect for a relaxed ride.
tips: Nice roundabout that we find in the Dos Mares roundabout where this CASA 101 - Aviojet plane was placed, ceded by the Ministry of Defense to the San Javier City Council. This plane flew over the sky of the Mar Menor for thirty years.
Nice and quiet road from Rebate to Cabezo de la Plata
Nice picture that we found rolling on the Murcian highway F-23 in good condition. The imposing Cabezo Gordo stands out, located at an altitude of 312 meters in the Campo de Cartagena.

faqs.question: What is the estimated cycling time for the Via Augusta and Sierras de Altaona Loop?
faqs.answer: The estimated cycling time for this 74 km racebike loop is approximately 4 hours and 13 minutes, based on an average pace. This duration can vary depending on your fitness level and how often you stop.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied, featuring mountainous sections with significant elevation gains. You can expect long, steady climbs and thrilling descents with fast, flowy curves. The route generally uses quiet roads with good surfaces, but be aware that some segments might transition to poorly maintained tarmac or even gravel, which can be challenging for a standard road bike.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the loop offers several points of interest. You'll pass by the CASA 101 Aviojet Monument at Dos Mares Roundabout, experience the scenic Rebate to Cabezo de la Plata Road, and enjoy a View of Cabezo Gordo. The route also provides panoramic views of rugged, pine-covered mountains and agricultural plains.

faqs.question: Is the Via Augusta and Sierras de Altaona Loop suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This loop is graded as moderate in difficulty. While it offers a rewarding experience, it involves significant elevation gains (over 600 meters) and varied terrain, requiring good to very good fitness. Beginners might find some climbs challenging, so it's best suited for cyclists with some experience.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Murcia region, where this loop is located, is known for its favorable climate. Cycling is generally pleasant outside of the peak summer months when temperatures can be very high. Spring and autumn offer ideal conditions with milder weather, making them the best times to enjoy the route comfortably.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Via Augusta and Sierras de Altaona Loop?
faqs.answer: The loop starts and ends near San Javier. While specific parking details for this exact route are not provided, you can typically find parking in towns like San Javier or San Pedro del Pinatar, which serve as common starting points for cycling in the area. Look for public parking areas or consult local maps for designated spots.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or permits required for cycling in the Sierras de Altaona area?
faqs.answer: The Sierras de Altaona are often associated with the ZEPA Monte El Valle Y Sierras De Altaona Y Escalona natural park. Generally, cycling on established roads and trails within natural parks is permitted, but it's always advisable to check for any specific local regulations regarding protected areas. There are typically no entrance fees for cycling on public roads within the park, but always respect signage and environmental guidelines.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a road cycling route, this loop is primarily designed for human cyclists. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, bringing a dog on a racebike route of this distance and difficulty is not recommended for their safety or comfort. If you wish to bring a dog, consider shorter, dedicated walking or gravel trails.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other official cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other official paths. You will encounter sections of the Via Augusta de la Comunitat Valenciana, Camino del Sureste - Ramal Sur, Transmurciana 01, Transmurciana 02, and Pilar Route 2, among others. These intersections are generally short segments of the overall loop.

faqs.question: Are there opportunities for refreshments or coffee stops along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the region is known for its cycling culture, and you can often find popular coffee stops in towns or villages along similar routes in Murcia. While specific establishments on this exact loop are not detailed, planning your ride to pass through towns will likely offer opportunities for breaks and refreshments.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect while cycling the loop?
faqs.answer: The loop offers diverse and engaging scenery. You'll experience panoramic views of rugged, pine-covered mountains, deep ravines, and extensive agricultural plains featuring vineyards, olive groves, and citrus orchards. The landscape shifts from dry, barren areas to forested climbs, providing a visually rich experience.

faqs.question: What should I be aware of regarding road surfaces on this route?
faqs.answer: While many sections feature quiet roads with generally good tarmac, be prepared for potential variations. Some areas may have poorly maintained tarmac or even short stretches of gravel. These sections can be less suitable for a standard road bike and might require extra caution or even dismounting and pushing your bike.
